content.s1_claims_discipline_internal:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# TAGS: lab anomaly, abnormal result, high value, low value, out of range, critical value, exogenous, medication effect, drug side effect, iatrogenic, supraphysiological, steroid, bodybuilding, polypharmacy, substance use, clinical reasoning, unexpected finding, contraindication, risk assessment, monitoring, safety check, pre-protocol screening, excess, overdose, too high, too low, dangerous level, extreme value, flag, concerning, unusual result, unexpected, doesn't make sense, weird labs, strange result, how to interpret, what to do when, anomaly detection, adaptive reasoning, context-dependent, patient-specific, not standard, not template, edge case, unusual case, complex case, special situation, override template, think first, reason before acting
# CATEGORY: Clinical Tools
# USE FOR: When labs show ANY value significantly out of range (high or low), when patient is on exogenous substances (hormones, steroids, medications that alter terrain), when standard template doesn't fit the patient, when clinical reasoning must override protocol
# COUPLED_SYSTEMS: 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION, 61_TERRAIN_ASSESSMENT, 17_MALE_HORMONES, 18_FEMALE_HORMONES, 54_DRUG_INTERACTIONS, 55_RED_FLAGS, 00_CRITICAL_REFERENCE, 65_BIOMARKER_ATLAS, 66_LAB_ANALYSIS_ENGINE
# RED_FLAGS: Any lab value >3x upper limit or <50% of lower limit, hematocrit >54% on exogenous testosterone, potassium >6.0, sodium <125, glucose <50 or >400, creatinine doubling from baseline
# TRIAGE_QUESTIONS: Are you taking any hormones, supplements, or medications? When were these labs drawn? Have you had recent labs for comparison?
# IV_ROLE: IV protocols may need dose adjustment, delay, or contraindication based on lab anomalies. Always check before proceeding.
# INTERVENTION_PRIORITY: Identify anomaly → Assess immediate risk → Order targeted follow-up → Correct urgent issues → THEN proceed with standard terrain/protocol
# TELECONSULT_LIMITS: CAN do remotely: lab interpretation, risk assessment, follow-up lab ordering, medication review. CANNOT do remotely: physical exam for related complications, urgent phlebotomy, emergency stabilization.
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CLINICAL REASONING — LAB ANOMALIES, EXOGENOUS SUBSTANCES & ADAPTIVE LOGIC
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 === SECTION 1: CLAIMS DISCIPLINE [INTERNAL_ONLY] ===
## WHY WE LOOK AT YOUR LABS BEFORE ANY PROTOCOL

Before starting any treatment, we review your lab results carefully — not 
just to check if things are "normal" or "abnormal," but to understand 
YOUR specific situation.

Sometimes a value that looks fine on paper tells us something important 
in context. And sometimes a value that's clearly out of range changes 
our entire approach.

Here's the principle: the treatment must fit YOUR body's current state, 
not a generic template. If something in your labs suggests your body 
isn't ready for a certain intervention, we address that first. It's 
not a delay — it's making sure the treatment actually works.

---

## WHEN YOUR LABS SHOW SOMETHING UNUSUAL

If we find a lab value that's significantly out of range, we don't ignore 
it and proceed with the standard plan. We:

1. Figure out WHY it's abnormal — is it a medication effect, a condition, 
   or something that needs investigation?
2. Assess if it creates any immediate risk that needs to be addressed first
3. Adjust our plan to work WITH your current biology, not against it
4. Order any additional tests needed to get the full picture
5. Then proceed with the protocol that fits YOUR situation

---

## IF YOU'RE TAKING HORMONES, STEROIDS, OR SPECIFIC MEDICATIONS

Certain substances change your body's baseline. Testosterone (TRT or 
higher doses), thyroid medication, insulin, corticosteroids, immunosuppressants 
— these all shift how your body responds to treatments.

We need to know about ALL substances you're taking — prescribed or not — 
because they affect what we can safely offer and how we adjust protocols. 
There's no judgment, just clinical necessity.

---

 === SECTION 2: PATIENT COMMUNICATION [PATIENT_OK] ===
## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
## CORE PRINCIPLE: REASON BEFORE APPLYING TEMPLATES
## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

The standard terrain checklist (61_TERRAIN_ASSESSMENT) detects DEFICIENCIES:
low FT3, low cortisol, low ferritin, low Hb, low Mg, high hsCRP, high homocysteine.

This file addresses what the standard checklist MISSES:

1. EXCESS / SUPRAPHYSIOLOGICAL VALUES — when something is too HIGH
2. EXOGENOUS SUBSTANCE EFFECTS — when medications/hormones shift the terrain
3. UNEXPECTED PATTERNS — when lab combinations don't fit standard profiles
4. CONTEXT-DEPENDENT INTERPRETATION — when the same value means different 
   things for different patients

## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
## STEP 1: LAB ANOMALY DETECTION — SCAN BEFORE TEMPLATING
## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

RULE: When labs are provided, BEFORE applying any template or checklist,
scan EVERY value against its reference range. Flag any value that is:
- Above the upper limit of normal
- Below the lower limit of normal
- At the extreme ends of normal (bottom or top 10%)
- Dramatically different from a previous value (if comparison available)

FOR EACH FLAGGED VALUE, ask three questions:
1. What are the IMMEDIATE RISKS of this specific anomaly?
2. What ADDITIONAL TESTS are needed because of this anomaly?
3. How does this anomaly CHANGE the standard protocol recommendation?

The answers to these questions OVERRIDE the standard terrain checklist.
Anomaly-driven tests come BEFORE routine terrain tests.

### Critical Thresholds — Require Immediate Action

| Parameter | Critical HIGH | Action | Critical LOW | Action |
|-----------|--------------|--------|-------------|--------|
| Hematocrit | >54% | URGENT: stroke/DVT/PE risk, phlebotomy, stop testosterone | <25% | URGENT: transfusion consideration, refer |
| Potassium | >6.0 mmol/L | URGENT: cardiac arrhythmia risk, ECG, refer | <3.0 mmol/L | URGENT: cardiac risk, supplement, refer |
| Sodium | >155 mmol/L | URGENT: dehydration, neurological risk | <125 mmol/L | URGENT: cerebral edema risk, refer |
| Glucose | >400 mg/dL | URGENT: DKA/HHS risk, refer | <50 mg/dL | URGENT: hypoglycemia, treat immediately |
| Creatinine | >2x baseline | URGENT: AKI possible, hold nephrotoxic agents | — | — |
| Calcium | >12 mg/dL | URGENT: hypercalcemia, investigate malignancy | <7 mg/dL | URGENT: seizure risk, supplement |
| Platelets | >600 K/uL | Risk: thrombosis, investigate cause | <50 K/uL | Risk: bleeding, hold procedures |
| WBC | >30 K/uL | Risk: leukemia, severe infection, refer | <2 K/uL | Risk: immunocompromised, refer |
| INR | >4.0 | Risk: bleeding, hold procedures | — | — |

### Significant Thresholds — Require Investigation Before Protocol

| Parameter | HIGH flag | What it means | Additional tests needed |
|-----------|----------|---------------|------------------------|
| Testosterone | >1200 ng/dL | Exogenous/supraphysiological | Hct, E2, LH/FSH, lipids (see 17_MALE_HORMONES) |
| Estradiol (men) | >50 pg/mL | Aromatization or exogenous | Testosterone, SHBG, liver function |
| TSH | >10 mIU/L | Overt hypothyroidism | FT3, FT4, anti-TPO, anti-TG |
| TSH | <0.1 mIU/L | Hyperthyroidism or over-medication | FT3, FT4, T3 total |
| Ferritin | >500 ng/mL | Iron overload or inflammation | Iron panel, hsCRP, transferrin sat, consider hemochromatosis |
| hsCRP | >10 mg/L | Active infection or severe inflammation | CBC, infection screening, ESR |
| MCV | >110 fL | Macrocytosis | B12, folate, reticulocyte count, consider alcohol/medication |
| MCV | <75 fL | Microcytosis | Iron panel, ferritin, hemoglobin electrophoresis |
| HOMA-IR | >5.0 | Severe insulin resistance | Fasting glucose, HbA1c, C-peptide, lipid panel |
| ALT/AST | >3x ULN | Liver stress | Full liver panel, GGT, hepatitis screen, medication review |
| eGFR | <30 mL/min | CKD Stage 4 | Cystatin C, urine ACR, electrolytes, adjust ALL drug doses |
| PSA | >4.0 ng/mL | Prostate investigation needed | Free PSA ratio, urology referral |
| Prolactin | >50 ng/mL | Pituitary investigation | MRI pituitary, medication review (antipsychotics raise prolactin) |

### Pattern Recognition — Combined Anomalies

| Pattern | Likely Cause | Investigate |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| High testosterone + Low HDL + High Hct | Exogenous testosterone (bodybuilder/TRT) | Full AAS monitoring panel |
| High MCV + Low B12 OR high MCV + normal B12 | B12 deficiency or folate deficiency or alcohol | Folate, MMA, reticulocytes |
| Low glucose + High insulin | Insulinoma or reactive hypoglycemia | C-peptide, 72hr fast if indicated |
| High ferritin + Normal/low iron saturation | Inflammation (not iron overload) | hsCRP, ESR — ferritin is acute phase reactant |
| High ferritin + High iron saturation (>45%) | True iron overload | HFE gene, transferrin sat, liver imaging |
| Low FT3 + Normal TSH + High rT3 | Sick euthyroid / T4-to-T3 conversion block | Cortisol, selenium, iodine, stress assessment |
| High cortisol + Low DHEA-S | HPA axis stress pattern / adrenal fatigue progression | 4-point cortisol, ACTH, circadian assessment |
| Low cortisol + Low DHEA-S | Adrenal insufficiency | Synacthen test, ACTH, endocrinology |
| Rising creatinine + Stable eGFR + High muscle mass | Creatinine rise from muscle, not kidney damage | Cystatin C (muscle-independent), urine ACR |
| Low albumin + High globulin | Chronic inflammation or liver dysfunction | Protein electrophoresis, liver panel |

## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
## STEP 2: EXOGENOUS SUBSTANCE LAYER — MEDICATIONS THAT SHIFT TERRAIN
## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

RULE: Before applying the standard terrain checklist, identify ALL 
exogenous substances the patient is taking. Each substance class 
has KNOWN effects on terrain that must be accounted for.

### Substance → Terrain Effect → Required Monitoring

| Substance Class | Known Terrain Effect | What to Monitor | Protocol Adjustment |
|----------------|---------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Exogenous testosterone (any dose)** | ↑Hct, ↓HDL, ↓LH/FSH, ↑E2, liver load | CBC q4-6w, lipids, E2, LH/FSH, PSA, liver | Lower NAD+ starting dose if supra; correct Hct before intensive IV |
| **Statins** | ↓CoQ10 (30-40%), muscle pain, ↑liver enzymes | CoQ10 level, CK if myalgia, liver panel | CoQ10 200mg MANDATORY; check mitochondrial function |
| **Metformin** | ↓B12 (10-30% of users), ↓folate, GI issues | B12, folate, MMA yearly | Supplement B12 1000mcg + folate |
| **PPIs (omeprazole etc.)** | ↓B12, ↓Mg, ↓Fe, ↓Ca absorption | B12, Mg RBC, ferritin, Ca, bone density | Supplement depleted nutrients; consider PPI weaning |
| **Insulin (exogenous)** | Hypoglycemia risk with ALA, weight gain | Glucose, HbA1c, CGM if available | CGM MANDATORY before ALA IV; adjust timing |
| **Corticosteroids (chronic)** | ↓HPA axis, ↑glucose, ↓bone, ↑infection risk | Cortisol AM (unreliable on steroids), DEXA, glucose | DO NOT interpret cortisol while on steroids; bone support |
| **Thyroid medication (T4/T3)** | Shifts all thyroid labs; ↑risk if overdosed | FT3, FT4, TSH, rT3 (timing critical: test before AM dose) | Test BEFORE morning dose; distinguish medication effect from conversion |
| **Immunosuppressants** | ↑Infection risk, ↓immune response, liver/kidney load | CBC, liver, kidney, infection screening | Limit immune-stimulating protocols; avoid high-dose immune IVs |
| **Anticoagulants (warfarin, DOACs)** | Bleeding risk, vitamin K interaction | INR/PT, avoid high-dose omega-3, limit Vit K fluctuation | Check before any injection; coordinate with prescriber |
| **SSRIs/SNRIs** | Serotonin syndrome risk with 5-HTP, SAMe, tryptophan | Mood, serotonin symptoms | NEVER combine with 5-HTP or SAMe without medical supervision |
| **Beta-blockers** | ↓HR, ↓exercise tolerance, fatigue, masks hypoglycemia | HR, BP, glucose (if diabetic), exercise tolerance | Adjust exercise protocols; cannot use HR for training zones |
| **Opioids (chronic)** | ↓Testosterone, ↓GnRH, ↓cortisol, constipation | Testosterone, cortisol, gut function | Test hormones; address opioid-induced hypogonadism |
| **Antibiotics (recent course)** | Gut dysbiosis, nutrient malabsorption | Gut symptoms, B12/folate, consider GI-MAP | Probiotic protocol; assess absorption before oral supplements |
| **Chemotherapy (current/recent)** | Immune suppression, organ toxicity, nutrient depletion | CBC, organ function per chemo protocol, nutrient levels | Coordinate with oncologist; see 25_ONCOLOGY_SUPPORT |
| **Diuretics** | ↓K, ↓Mg, ↓Na, dehydration, ↑uric acid | Electrolytes, Mg RBC, uric acid | Replete electrolytes before IV; adjust hydration |

### The Stacking Effect

Patients often take MULTIPLE substances that compound terrain shifts:

Example — Common elderly patient:
Statin (↓CoQ10) + PPI (↓B12, ↓Mg) + Metformin (↓B12) + Diuretic (↓Mg, ↓K)
= TRIPLE B12 depletion + DOUBLE Mg depletion + CoQ10 deficit
= Fatigue, brain fog, muscle weakness — often attributed to "aging"

Example — Bodybuilder:
Testosterone implant (↑Hct, ↓HDL) + protein supplements + gym training
= Polycythemia risk + cardiovascular risk + dehydration risk during IV
= Standard NAD+ protocol contraindicated until Hct verified

ALWAYS calculate the CUMULATIVE effect of all substances before recommending.

## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
## STEP 3: ADAPTIVE REASONING — WHEN TEMPLATES DON'T FIT
## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

The terrain checklist (61_TERRAIN_ASSESSMENT) is a STARTING POINT, not a 
COMPLETE ASSESSMENT. It catches the most common issues. This file catches 
what it misses.

### When to Override the Standard Template

OVERRIDE TRIGGER 1: Any lab value >2x the upper limit of normal
→ This value becomes PRIORITY ONE. Standard terrain tests become secondary.
→ The tests to order are driven by the RISKS of this specific anomaly.
→ Example: Testosterone 2947 ng/dL → hematocrit is now #1 priority, not folate.

OVERRIDE TRIGGER 2: Patient on exogenous substances that shift terrain
→ The substance effects layer (Step 2) is applied BEFORE the terrain checklist.
→ Known depletions/excesses from substances are tested FIRST.
→ Example: Patient on statin → CoQ10 level before mitochondrial protocol.

OVERRIDE TRIGGER 3: Lab combination that suggests a specific diagnosis
→ Pattern recognition (Step 1) may reveal a diagnosis that changes everything.
→ The diagnosis-specific workup takes priority over generic terrain.
→ Example: Ferritin 800 + transferrin sat 55% → hemochromatosis workup before any iron-related protocol.

OVERRIDE TRIGGER 4: Previous labs available for comparison
→ TREND matters more than a single value.
→ A creatinine of 1.5 that was 0.9 six months ago = acute change = urgent.
→ A creatinine of 1.5 that's been 1.5 for 3 years = stable CKD = different approach.

OVERRIDE TRIGGER 5: Patient age changes interpretation
→ "Normal" ranges include all ages. Optimal ranges are age-adjusted.
→ Testosterone 365 ng/dL at 35 years old = potentially low for age.
→ Testosterone 365 ng/dL at 75 years old = reasonable for age.
→ DHEA-S 164 at 35 = bottom of range (concerning). At 70 = expected.

### The Reasoning Hierarchy

When labs are provided, Valentine's reasoning order is:

1. SCAN all values — flag anything out of range or at extremes
2. CHECK for exogenous substances — apply substance effects layer
3. LOOK for patterns — combined anomalies that suggest specific causes
4. COMPARE to previous labs — assess trend if available
5. IDENTIFY IMMEDIATE RISKS — what needs action NOW vs. what can wait
6. ORDER TARGETED TESTS — driven by anomalies, not by template
7. THEN apply standard terrain checklist — for anything not already covered
8. ADJUST PROTOCOL — based on all of the above, not generic recommendation

This is the difference between a protocol-follower and a clinician.

## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
## STEP 4: COMMUNICATION — HOW TO EXPLAIN ANOMALIES TO PATIENTS
## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

When explaining lab anomalies to patients:

DO:
- State the finding clearly: "Your testosterone is at 2947 — that's about 
  3.5 times the normal maximum"
- Explain the risk simply: "At this level, your blood can become too thick, 
  which increases clot risk"
- Give the immediate action: "We need to check your hematocrit before 
  anything else"
- Connect to their protocol: "Once we know that's safe, we can adjust 
  the IV plan to fit your current biology"

DON'T:
- Panic them: "This is extremely dangerous"
- Lecture them: "You shouldn't be taking this"
- Ignore it: Proceed with standard template as if everything is normal
- Over-explain: Dump every possible risk and mechanism at once

TONE: Matter-of-fact, clinical, solution-oriented. Not judgmental.

---

 === SECTION 3: PHARMACOKINETICS & DYNAMICS (ADME) [INTERNAL_ONLY] ===
## LAB ANOMALY DECISION FLOWCHART

```
LABS PROVIDED
     │
     ▼
SCAN ALL VALUES against reference ranges
     │
     ├── Any value >2x upper limit? ──YES──→ ANOMALY PROTOCOL (this file)
     │                                         - Identify immediate risks
     │                                         - Order anomaly-specific tests FIRST
     │                                         - THEN terrain checklist
     │
     ├── Patient on exogenous substances? ──YES──→ SUBSTANCE LAYER (Step 2)
     │                                              - Known effects on terrain
     │                                              - Substance-specific monitoring
     │                                              - THEN terrain checklist
     │
     ├── Unusual combination/pattern? ──YES──→ PATTERN RECOGNITION (Step 1)
     │                                          - Specific diagnosis workup
     │                                          - THEN terrain checklist
     │
     └── All values within range ──→ STANDARD TERRAIN CHECKLIST (61_TERRAIN_ASSESSMENT)
```

## QUICK LOOKUP: COMMON SUBSTANCES & WHAT TO CHECK

| Substance | CHECK FIRST |
|-----------|-------------|
| Testosterone (any) | Hct, E2, LH/FSH, HDL, PSA |
| Statin | CoQ10, CK if symptoms, liver |
| Metformin | B12, folate |
| PPI | B12, Mg, Fe, Ca |
| Insulin | Glucose/CGM before ALA |
| Corticosteroids | Don't trust cortisol labs; check glucose, bone |
| SSRI/SNRI | No 5-HTP/SAMe; check sodium (SIADH risk) |
| Warfarin/DOAC | INR; no high-dose omega-3/Vit K changes |
| Opioids (chronic) | Testosterone, cortisol, gut |
| Diuretics | K, Mg, Na, uric acid |

## QUICK LOOKUP: EXTREME VALUES & IMMEDIATE ACTION

| Finding | DO THIS FIRST |
|---------|---------------|
| Hct >54% | URGENT: stop testosterone, phlebotomy, refer |
| K >6.0 | URGENT: ECG, refer |
| Na <125 | URGENT: refer, fluid restrict |
| Glucose <50 | URGENT: treat hypo |
| Glucose >400 | URGENT: DKA/HHS screen, refer |
| Testosterone >1200 | Check Hct, E2, LH/FSH, lipids — NOT folate/homocysteine |
| Ferritin >500 | Check iron sat, hsCRP — is it iron or inflammation? |
| TSH <0.1 | Check FT3/FT4 — hyperthyroid or overmedication? |
| MCV >110 | Check B12, folate, reticulocytes — not just CBC |
| hsCRP >10 | Active infection? Rule out before chronic inflammation protocol |

---

CEPAT SEHAT — Integrative & Regenerative Medicine
Bali • Jakarta • Surabaya • Phuket
